items.quote: He did not see anything in the demo that proved to him that Zycus could do anything that we weren't currently able to do.
That's exactly this use case, and the more they train some of your organization... we'll be able to float that up in 10 seconds versus 10 days of your time.
items.source: Avoma call 2025-09-09 (CLM Demo Test run with Jordan)

items.area: Close date credibility vs. RFP timeline
items.quote: 6/29 - ARUP finally released their RFP for CLM with the following due dates... Proposal Submission Deadline: 07/10/2026.

sources: Avoma call 2025-09-09 (CLM Demo Test run with Jordan)
metrics.narrative: The champion has repeatedly articulated qualitative pain (manual redlining in Salesforce/Conga, version-control chaos, no risk scoring, data scattered across '7 different places') across multiple calls, most explicitly on 2025-10-16 ('right now, we have to go 7 different places to do those things... that's a pain'). No dollar-quantified savings or ROI figure has been buyer-voiced; the metrics case remains qualitative, not quantified.

sources: Avoma call 2025-09-09 (CLM Demo Test run with Jordan)
champion.narrative: Michael Tutton is a strong, long-tenured champion: described on the 2025-09-09 call as having 'been our champion for 5 or 6 years' and stating on 2025-10-16, 'I have to have a tool that will not an option... it's kind of the hill I'm gonna die on.' He personally drove management to approve the CLM RFP that finally released on 2026-06-29 (Next Step, 6/1 and 5/26 entries: 'his efforts are finally paying off... he has convinced management to kick off an RFP for CLM'). He is at risk, however: despite 14 months of advocacy he has not yet converted the skeptical director-level reviewer, and the org's own Salesforce/Conga team continues to build a competing enhancement case in parallel (call 2026-01-09, 2026-02-04).

sources: Avoma call 2025-08-06 (Zycus X ARUP - Initial Demo)
competition.narrative: The incumbent is Conga on Salesforce, which the champion wants to replace ('I don't wanna just spend more money... I wanna save money on development and the Conga tool licensing,' call 2025-09-08) but which the org's own Conga-aligned internal team is actively defending by building a gap-analysis / enhancement case with leadership (three internal meetings completed by 2026-01-09, ongoing as of 2026-02-04). Coupa was named and dismissed as lacking robust contract management (call 2025-09-03). DocuSign was raised only as an e-signature feature comparison against Certinal (call 2025-08-06), not as a full competitor. With the RFP now live, unnamed RFP co-bidders will enter the field once proposals are evaluated.

sources: Avoma call 2025-08-06 (Zycus X ARUP - Initial Demo)
identify_pain.narrative: Pain is extensively and consistently documented across nine months of calls: entirely manual contract authoring and redlining via email and ad-hoc Salesforce/Conga versioning (call 2025-08-06), version-control confusion between simultaneous vs. sequential review (call 2025-09-03), no clause library or risk scoring in the current toolset (call 2025-09-08), and a homegrown 'eBuy' intake system plus disrupted D365 rollout (call 2025-05-06). The champion has personally lived this pain for years and it is the basis of his advocacy.

paper_process.narrative: No paper process has started. The deal has not reached commercial agreement or contracting; the RFP proposal itself (due 2026-07-10) has not yet been submitted, and per-user/per-envelope pricing is still being finalized. Nothing on legal, MSA, or SOW has been raised in any call or Next Step entry.

sources: Avoma call 2025-10-06 (Prep with Zycus for CLM Demo)
economic_buyer.narrative: No confirmed economic buyer exists in Salesforce. Calls point to an unverified director-level stakeholder who reviewed the initial CLM demo and told the team he 'did not see anything... that proved Zycus could do anything we weren't currently able to do' (call 2025-09-08), and who remained 'very skeptical' as of 2025-10-16 despite the champion's continued advocacy. This person's identity has never been matched to a Salesforce contact and is not named here per that gap. Arthur Moes (Group Manager, Purchasing & Material Planning) also declined an invite to a follow-up demo (call 2025-10-06) and has shown no direct engagement since.

decision_process.narrative: A real process now exists for the first time: RFP intent-to-respond, questions, and a Proposal Submission Deadline of 2026-07-10 (tomorrow), plus a buyer-reserved IT security questionnaire/system evaluation for shortlisted vendors after proposals are reviewed. Internally, ARUP has been running a parallel evaluation of enhancing its incumbent Salesforce/Conga stack, with three rounds of internal meetings completed with the Conga team as of 2026-01-09 and continuing as of 2026-02-04 ('still doing evaluation... still gonna be a hard sell'). The process is real but its timeline does not reconcile with the recorded close date.

sources: Avoma call 2025-10-13 (Zycus X ARUP - CLM Demo)
decision_criteria.narrative: Criteria are now structurally defined by the formal RFP for CLM released 2026-06-29, which requires a full technical, functional, and commercial response, and specifically calls out per-user and per-envelope pricing across iContract, Certinal, and supplier-risk modules. Prior to the RFP, criteria were informally gathered across the demo cycle: redlining/version control, clause library, external Word collaboration, D365/Salesforce integration, and risk scoring (calls 2025-09-09, 2025-10-13). The formal RFP scoring detail itself (weighting, must-haves) is not yet visible to us.

engagement.top_event: [Clari - Email Sent] Re: Zycus: RFP – Consolidated Responses to Submitted Questions

items.name: ARUP sends consolidated RFP Q&A to all suppliers
items.summary: Mzia Sturua (ARUP IT PMO) distributed a consolidated document of all questions and answers submitted by RFP participants, formally incorporating them into the RFP documentation and inviting further questions via reply.

items.name: Steve acknowledges receipt
items.summary: Steve Ovadje replied with a brief thank-you confirming receipt of the consolidated Q&A doc — no further questions raised, closing the loop on this exchange for now.

day_summary.overall: ARUP's PMO sent all RFP participants a consolidated Q&A document to keep the competitive process fair and transparent, formally rolling those clarifications into the RFP documentation. Steve simply acknowledged receipt, so the ball is now in ARUP's court to run the rest of the RFP timeline — no open asks on our side beyond monitoring for further updates.

cro_panel.intro: A years-long champion has finally forced a formal RFP into existence after more than a year of internal delay, which is real, buyer-driven progress, but the deal is entering the RFP with no confirmed economic buyer, an unresolved internal build-vs-buy fight against the incumbent, and a close date that cannot mathematically fit the buyer's own stated evaluation steps

bullets.text: No confirmed economic buyer; MEDDPICC marks EB a gap; calls reference only an unverified director-level reviewer who hasn't been identified in Salesforce
Incumbent actively defended; Conga/Salesforce is running its own internal gap-analysis in parallel to the Zycus RFP (per competition notes, as of early Feb 2026)
Long-tenured champion delivered the RFP; Michael Tutton, a 5-7 year Zycus advocate, converted management to formally kick off the CLM RFP (5/26, 6/1 notes)
Close-date credibility broken; pushed at least 12 times since the original 12/31/2025 target, most recently to 7/31/2026, undercutting confidence in the 'Shortlisted' timeline
Champion flags uphill fight; states displacing the Conga/Salesforce incumbent will be 'a hard sell'; skeptical director-level reviewer still not converted
Deep historical engagement; 13 fully transcribed meetings (Aug 2025-Feb 2026) show real multi-session buyer participation in demos, though none since 2/04/2026

blocks.read: Lukewarm — some motion, but not strong.
blocks.title: Deal momentum

bullets.text: RFP finally live; ARUP released the CLM RFP on 6/29/2026, with a 7/10/2026 proposal deadline, after 14 months of internal delay per the next-step log
No buyer meeting in 155 days; last recorded call was 2/04/2026; zero meetings logged in the 30/60/90-day windows
Close date slipping; trend shows a 31-day push (6/30→7/31/2026), part of a pattern of at least 12 slips since the original 12/31/2025 target
Champion still driving cadence; Michael Tutton personally convinced management to launch the RFP (5/26 and 6/1 next-step notes), keeping the deal moving after months of stall
No future meeting on calendar; next_scheduled is null; all forward motion now rides on ARUP's own paper deadlines, not a booked conversation
Forecast unchanged despite kickoff; still 'Upside Key Deal,' not moved to Best Case/Commit even after the RFP release

blocks.title: What could lose it

items: Finalize and submit the RFP proposal, including locked per-user/per-envelope pricing, before the deadline. (by 10 Jun)
Confirm proposal receipt with the champion and clarify the IT security questionnaire timeline for shortlisted vendors. (by 14 Jun)
Propose a realistic mutual close plan that reflects the RFP's own post-proposal review steps instead of the current 2026-07-31 date. (by 20 Jun)

scope_change.to: A formal RFP scoped specifically to contract lifecycle management (CLM)
scope_change.from: Broad, multi-module S2P conversations (CLM, supplier management, eProcurement, sourcing) across the 2025 demo cycle
scope_change.detail: The buyer's RFP, released 2026-06-29, is explicitly for CLM; broader supplier-management and eProcurement conversations from 2025 (e.g., 2025-10-16 iSupplier discussion) have not been formally re-raised in the RFP scope.

ai_fit_signal.summary: The buyer has repeatedly probed AI mechanics (how Merlin trains, whether it's 'a specifically designed chat GTP', whether AI capability can be bought standalone) without yet committing to AI as a primary driver; interest is real but still exploratory.

business_case.evidence: Pain is well documented qualitatively (manual redlining, version-control chaos, data scattered across systems — calls 2025-08-06, 2025-09-03, 2025-10-16) but no dollar-quantified ROI or savings figure has been voiced by the buyer.

items.change: Buyer confirmed attendance for a Procurement Pinnacle roundtable

deal_movement.summary: After roughly 14 months of intermittent, largely stalled engagement (repeated 'no material update' entries from February through May 2026), the buyer formally released a CLM RFP on 2026-06-29 that names Zycus as an included vendor, with a Proposal Submission Deadline of 2026-07-10.

forecast_read.math: days_to_close=22 vs proposal due in 1 day + IT security review (unscoped, buyer-reserved for shortlisted vendors) + selection + paper process not yet started; no economic buyer confirmed to authorize a fast close.
forecast_read.reason: The RFP the buyer released on 2026-06-29 sets its own Proposal Submission Deadline at 2026-07-10 (tomorrow) and explicitly reserves an IT security questionnaire/system evaluation for shortlisted vendors AFTER proposals are in, with award/selection to follow. The recorded CloseDate of 2026-07-31 (22 days out) cannot accommodate proposal review, an IT security round, vendor selection, and contracting in the remaining window. This close date has already been pushed 12+ times from an original 2025-12-31 target; the pattern continues.

ceo_intervention.summary: Scope shrinking vs prior — The buyer's RFP, released 2026-06-29, is explicitly for CLM; broader supplier-management and eProcurement conversations from 2025 (e.g., 2025-10-16 iSupplier discussion) have not been formally

critical_signals.text: The incumbent's own internal team has spent multiple sessions building a case to enhance the existing tool instead of replacing it, and that internal build-vs-buy fight is still unresolved as of early February 2026, even after the RFP formally opened at the end of June 2026.

The buyer finally released its formal RFP for contract management on 29 June 2026 (about 10 days ago) after roughly 14 months of internal delay, but the proposal deadline it set is tomorrow, 10 July 2026, while the recorded close date sits three weeks out on 31 July, leaving no visible room for the security review the buyer itself reserved for shortlisted vendors.

The RFP requires granular per-user and per-envelope pricing across the contract management, e-signature, and supplier-risk modules to be finalized ahead of tomorrow's deadline, and that pricing has not yet been locked.

The senior reviewer who was unconvinced by an early demo, and whose identity has never been confirmed in Salesforce, remains the deal's real unresolved objection: he has not been shown updated proof points since the RFP went live.

confidence_signals.summary: A genuine, buyer-driven RFP release and a verified recent two-way email exchange show the deal is live, but coverage of the last five months (Feb-Jun 2026) rests mostly on Next Step notes rather than fresh calls, and the economic buyer remains unresolved.

north_star_verdict.math: Opportunity created 2025-07-25 (~347 days open as of 2026-07-07). Close date 2026-07-31 is only 24 days away, but the RFP proposal Zycus must submit isn't due until 2026-07-10 (~3 days from today), and the RFP itself reserves a further IT security questionnaire and evaluation step for shortlisted vendors AFTER proposals are in. None of that fits inside the remaining 21 days after submission, so the close date will slip regardless of how the RFP result lands.
evidence: SF Next_Step__c updated 2026-06-29: RFP released with Intent to Respond 6/9, Questions 6/16, Proposal due 7/10/2026
SF CloseDate 2026-07-31 vs proposal deadline 2026-07-10 leaves no time for post-proposal evaluation
north_star_verdict.headline: Michael Tutton's year-long internal campaign finally landed a formal RFP with Zycus guaranteed a seat, but the 31 Jul 2026 close date cannot survive a process whose own proposal deadline (10 Jul 2026) hasn't been met yet.
north_star_verdict.recommended_forecast: Pipeline (push close date to Q4 2026 at the earliest, pending RFP scoring, security review, and contracting)

flags: With 'several participants' confirmed in ARUP's live RFP (24 Jun 2026), differentiate on AI-assisted simultaneous review and redlining (Merlin) rather than price, since Conga's core documented gap is manual, non-simultaneous review.

Equip champion Michael Tutton with a quantified ROI/savings case before the 10 Jul 2026 proposal, since he has already flagged expecting 'a hard sell' internally and currently has only qualitative pain points to argue with.

No economic buyer or final decision-maker is confirmed in Salesforce this late in the cycle; use the RFP's own promised post-proposal 'demonstrations, reference checks' phase to get in front of the real approver and name them in Salesforce.

ARUP's own Conga/Salesforce team is pitching an in-house enhancement path in parallel to the RFP; the proposal must quantify what Conga structurally cannot do (redlining, risk scoring, D365-integrated obligation reporting), not rely on live demos alone.
No Salesforce-verified economic buyer sits above the sourcing team; push for a review with the director evaluating build-vs-buy before the proposal lands so the decision isn't made on price alone.

Reinforce Zycus's Gartner Magic Quadrant placement (shared with Michael Tutton in Jan 2026) directly inside the RFP response, since it was explicitly flagged as a credibility lever for Michael's internal sell.
Neutralize the internal build-vs-buy fight directly: ARUP's own Salesforce/Conga team has completed multiple internal gap-analysis rounds (through 2026-02-04) that compete with the RFP itself.
Establish a credible mutual timeline: propose a realistic close date to replace 2026-07-31, one that actually accounts for the RFP's own reserved IT security review for shortlisted vendors.
best_practice_check.summary: The RFP finally moves this deal forward, but the win depends on resolving three long-standing structural gaps rather than more demos.

customer_preference.evidence: Champion Michael Tutton continues to push Zycus exclusively and secured its inclusion in the CLM RFP (Next Step, 2026-06-01), but the senior reviewer's skepticism (2025-09-08, 2025-10-16) and the parallel internal Conga-enhancement evaluation (2026-01-09, 2026-02-04) show real, unresolved internal division rather than a clear buyer-wide preference.

competitive_position.summary: Conga (the incumbent, native to Salesforce) is the dominant and most current threat: it is being actively defended by ARUP's own internal team through a formal gap-analysis exercise with leadership, running in parallel to the Zycus RFP as of early February 2026. Coupa and DocuSign are minor, dated, non-current mentions. Unnamed RFP co-bidders will enter the field now that the RFP has formally opened.

competitors.name: Conga
competitors.quote: What can we change in Salesforce Conga or enhance or whatever to get to where we feel like we need to be, and how does that compare to the Zycus tool?
competitors.source: Avoma Catch up with Zycus, 9 Jan 2026
competitors.how_we_win: Continue arming the champion with differentiated proof points (redlining, risk scoring, Word-native collaboration) that Conga structurally cannot match, since the internal Conga team's own gap analysis is the deciding forum.

competitors.name: unnamed RFP co-bidders
competitors.quote: 6/29 - ARUP finally released their RFP for CLM with the following due dates.
competitors.how_we_win: No names known yet; ensure the proposal response is submitted and differentiated before other bidders are seen.

competitors.name: Coupa
competitors.quote: Coupa was mentioned as a competitor; however, it lacks the robust contract management functionality offered by Zycus.
competitors.how_we_win: No action needed; not re-raised since.

competitors.name: DocuSign CLM
competitors.quote: Send over a comparison document between DocuSign and Certinal along with case studies regarding CLM relevant to the industry.
competitors.source: Avoma Zycus X ARUP - Initial Demo, 6 Aug 2025
competitors.how_we_win: Positioning is feature-comparison only (e-signature), not a full-suite threat; keep Certinal materials current if requested again.

deal_scores_evidence.summary: A years-long champion has finally forced a formal RFP into existence after more than a year of internal delay, which is real, buyer-driven progress, but the deal is entering the RFP with no confirmed economic buyer, an unresolved internal build-vs-buy fight against the incumbent, and a close date that cannot mathematically fit the buyer's own stated evaluation steps.

deal_risk.text: Close date not credible — 2026-07-31 leaves no room for the RFP's own reserved IT security questionnaire for shortlisted vendors, given the proposal itself isn't due until 2026-07-10; this close date has already slipped 12+ times from an original 2025-12-31 target.
Build-vs-buy political risk persists into the RFP — even a technical win in the RFP does not resolve the parallel internal case to simply enhance the existing Salesforce/Conga tool.

win_position.text: Champion finally landed the RFP — after 14 months of stalling, Michael Tutton got management approval to launch a formal CLM RFP that released on 2026-06-29, with Zycus explicitly included (Next Step, 2026-06-01).

No confirmed economic buyer — the only senior reviewer on record was unconvinced by the initial demo on 2025-09-08 ('did not see anything that proved Zycus could do anything we weren't currently able to do') and remains unverified in Salesforce.

Incumbent actively defended internally — ARUP's own Salesforce/Conga team completed three rounds of internal gap-analysis meetings by 2026-01-09 and was still evaluating as of 2026-02-04, meaning Zycus is competing against an internal enhancement case, not just an external RFP field.

deal_momentum.text: Genuine two-way buyer contact 15 days ago — a buyer email reply was logged 2026-06-24, and the buyer's own RFP release (2026-06-29) is a real deliverable landing, not a rep-side update.
Commercial ask under time pressure — the RFP requires finalized per-user/per-envelope pricing across three modules by the 2026-07-10 deadline (tomorrow), and that pricing is not yet locked.

customer_commitment.text: Champion has publicly staked his position — Michael Tutton said replacing the incumbent is 'the hill I'm gonna die on' (call 2025-10-16), and personally drove the RFP approval.
Champion's own words flag internal difficulty — he called this 'a hard sell' internally as recently as 2026-02-04, and the org is still running a parallel Conga-enhancement evaluation.

items.grounding_quote: ARUP's RFP demands granular per-user and per-envelope pricing across iContract, Certinal, and supplier risk modules ahead of the 2026-07-10 deadline; commercial proposal not yet finalized.

ai_positioning_strength.summary: Zycus's AI (Merlin risk scoring, Negotiation Genie, Obligation Extraction Genie) initially confused the buyer during a rushed early demo (2025-09-03: 'confusion regarding how Merlin gathers data... in contrast to ChatGPT and Copilot'), but a subsequent, better-scripted demo on 2025-10-13 landed the AI-driven risk scoring and metadata extraction clearly.

items.quote: The ability for an external person to take a document... and have it pull up in Word... that is a game changer for our legal team.
Here's your inadequacies, and here's what you should redline first right off the bat.
items.source: Avoma call 2025-10-06 (Prep with Zycus for CLM Demo)

items.quote: He did not see anything in the demo that proved to him that Zycus could do anything that we weren't currently able to do.
items.criterion: Overall demo differentiation vs. current toolset (initial attempt)

customer_expectations_fit.summary: The buyer's stated needs center on redlining/version control, a clause library, risk scoring, external Word-based legal collaboration, D365/Salesforce integration, and (secondarily) supplier risk — all repeatedly demonstrated across the 2025 demo cycle, though an early demo (2025-09-03) failed to land the differentiation clearly.
